coredata.dc:title: High anti-corrosive and Scalable CDs/BCN heterostructure photocatalysts for efficient H2 production in natural seawater
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Yaoze
Huang, Jun
Zhang, Zhi
Kang, Zihu
Tao, Xia
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic natural seawater-derived splitting to produce hydrogen is one of the most potential strategies to alleviate the shortage of freshwater resources and energy crisis. Nevertheless, the complicated composition of natural seawater poses substantial challenges to the activity and stability of photocatalysts. Here, we report on the fabrication of carbon dots (CDs) and B elements co-modified carbon nitride (i.e. CDs/BCN) via precursor-mediated supramolecular self-assembly and subsequent NaBH4-assisted thermal reduction as well as its implementation as seawater-derived H2 production. The fabricated CDs/BCN exhibits the flaky porous structure capable of providing more transport channels for the generated gases (e.g. H2) and precipitates (e.g. Mg(OH)2, Ca(OH)2) during the seawater splitting process. Benefitting from cooperation of CDs and B elements i.e. the inhibited recombination of electron-hole pairs as well as the extended optical absorption range of CN, the optimal CDs/BCN-2 under visible-light irradiation exhibits the high H2 production rate up to 10369 μmol g-1 h-1 in natural seawater. The proven satisfactory reusability and the corresponding structural stability demonstrate the excellent corrosion resistance of CDs/BCN-2 in natural seawater. Additionally, CDs/BCN-20 photocatalyst synthesized via 20-fold precursor amplification procedure still displays efficient photocatalytic performance of 9022 μmol g-1 h-1, indicating its potential for industrial applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphitic carbon nitride
Carbon dots
Boron elements
Natural seawater
Photocatalytic hydrogen production

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthetic process for CDs/BCN photocatalyst.
(a) SEM image, (b) TEM image, (c–e) HRTEM images and (f–j) EDS elemental mapping images of CDs/BCN-2.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of CN, CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-x.
High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s, (c) B 1s for CN, CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-2 and (d) solid-state 11B NMR spectrum of CDs/BCN-2.
(a) UV–vis DRS, (b) Tauc plots, (c) high-resolution valence band XPS spectra and (d) energy band structure diagrams of CN, CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-2.
(a) Transient photocurrent response curves, (b) EIS plots, (c) steady-state PL spectra and (d) time-resolved PL spectra based on CN, CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-2 (Inset table in (d): PL decay fitting data).

(a) Time-dependent H2 production curves and (b) H2 production rates of CN, CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-x in natural seawater, (c) H2 production rates of previously reported g-C3N4-based photocatalysts under simulated sunlight irradiation, (d) comparison of photocatalytic performance of CDs/BCN-2 in different salt solutions, (e) wavelength-dependent AQY for H2 production of CDs/BCN-2, (f) cycling stability test of CDs/BCN-2, (g) XRD patterns and (h) FT-IR spectra of CDs/BCN-2 before and after the cycling stability test.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) FT-IR spectra and (c) photocatalytic H2 production rates for CN, CDs/BCN-2 and CDs/BCN-20 and (d) cycling stability test of CDs/BCN-20 in natural seawater.
Proposed mechanism for the photocatalytic H2 production in natural seawater over CDs/BCN-2.

head.ce:title: High anti-corrosive and Scalable CDs/BCN heterostructure photocatalysts for efficient H2 production in natural seawater

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic natural seawater-derived splitting to produce hydrogen is one of the most potential strategies to alleviate the shortage of freshwater resources and energy crisis. Nevertheless, the complicated composition of natural seawater poses substantial challenges to the activity and stability of photocatalysts. Here, we report on the fabrication of carbon dots (CDs) and B elements co-modified carbon nitride (i.e. CDs/BCN) via precursor-mediated supramolecular self-assembly and subsequent NaBH4-assisted thermal reduction as well as its implementation as seawater-derived H2 production. The fabricated CDs/BCN exhibits the flaky porous structure capable of providing more transport channels for the generated gases (e.g. H2) and precipitates (e.g. Mg(OH)2, Ca(OH)2) during the seawater splitting process. Benefitting from cooperation of CDs and B elements i.e. the inhibited recombination of electron-hole pairs as well as the extended optical absorption range of CN, the optimal CDs/BCN-2 under visible-light irradiation exhibits the high H2 production rate up to 10369 μmol g-1 h-1 in natural seawater. The proven satisfactory reusability and the corresponding structural stability demonstrate the excellent corrosion resistance of CDs/BCN-2 in natural seawater. Additionally, CDs/BCN-20 photocatalyst synthesized via 20-fold precursor amplification procedure still displays efficient photocatalytic performance of 9022 μmol g-1 h-1, indicating its potential for industrial applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen (H2) energy, regarded as one of the most prospective renewable energy resources, can be used to replace conventional fossil fuels to solve severe environmental pollution and energy shortage problems [1]. Among numerous H2 production methods, photocatalytic conversion of solar energy to H2 energy has gained increasing attention owing to the environmental benign, low resources consumption and low cost [2]. Up to date, most of the work associated with photocatalytic H2 production mainly focuses in the pure water system [3–6]. Although water resource is very abundant on the earth, 97.5% of water resource is highly saline seawater while only 2.5% is fresh water [7]. In this case, photocatalytic splitting natural seawater to produce H2 is regarded as a more valuable and feasible replacement. Worthy noted, natural seawater contains the complicated components, where various microorganisms, impurities and ions coexist, especially large amounts of metal ions (such as Mg2+ and Ca2+), tend to form precipitates on the catalyst surface resulting in the decrease of its hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) activity [8]. In addition, H2 production from natural seawater is often accompanied by the evolution of Cl2, which can corrode the catalyst and thus reduce its durability [9]. Thus, seeking high-activity stable H2-production photocatalysts adapted to natural seawater remains a prominent focus and challenge.

Through the unremitting efforts of researchers, various semiconductor photocatalysts such as g-C3N4, TiO2 and CdS have been exploited to achieve efficient photocatalytic H2 production in natural seawater [10–12]. A 2D metal-free polymer i.e. graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) with the merits of excellent corrosion resistance in natural seawater, suitable energy band structure, simple synthesis method and low toxicity, has protruded from the above current-photocatalysts [13]. However, unmodified CN still suffers from low specific surface area, poor visible-light utilization and severe carriers recombination, which greatly hinders its application in photocatalytic conversion of solar energy to H2. To address these issues, several strategies with notable results have been proposed in recent researches, either element doping, defect introduction or heterojunction construction [14–16]. Among them, anchoring electron acceptors such as CDs, MoS2 and MoSe2 on the g-C3N4 surface can remarkably accelerate the photo-generated electrons capture and thus the reduction of H+ [17–21]. For example, Lu and his coworkers confirmed experimentally and theoretically that CDs loaded on g-C3N4 surface not only increased the charge separation and transfer efficiency but also provided additional reactive sites for HER, thus realizing a high photocatalytic activity in simulated seawater i.e. 3.5 wt% NaCl solution under full spectrum light irradiation [22]. Although the concentration of 3.5 wt% NaCl solution is extremely close to that of natural seawater, it has never been able to simulate the complex environment of natural seawater. Therefore, the photocatalytic activity of CDs modified g-C3N4 under visible-light irradiation in real natural seawater still remains to be explored.

Doping non-metallic elements (e.g. B, C, P and S) into g-C3N4 is also considered as a simple and harmless two-for-one way to simultaneously achieve the atomic-level modulation for specific optical and electrical properties of g-C3N4 and eliminate the environmental pollution caused by the leakage of doped metallic elements [23–25]. Particularly, a safe and non-polluting boron (B) element introduced to g-C3N4 has been proven to tune its electron density thus leading to stronger light absorption and faster spatial charge transport [26,27]. Besides, the B–N bond introduced via boron dopants offers more transfer channels for diverting electrons and energy to the g-C3N4 surface, and thus more favorable for redox reactions [28]. These proven merits of B doped g-C3N4, together with the inherent stability of g-C3N4 itself in natural seawater, are anticipated to increase photo-response and charge transfer and hence improve the natural seawater-derived photocatalytic performance.

In this context, we herein construct dual non-metallic doping of CDs and B elements on g-C3N4, named as CDs/BCN, through a simple two-step calcination method with the aim of improving its photocatalytic performance in natural seawater. This designed CDs/BCN exhibits flaky porous structure that allows enabling faster mass transfer for HER. The spectroscopic and electrochemical assays demonstrate that the introduction of CDs and B elements effectively expedites the separation and transfer of photo-generated carriers and enhances the visible-light capture capacity. Under optimal conditions, the CDs/BCN-2 displays outstanding visible-light-induced catalytic activity up to 10369 μmol g-1 h-1 in natural seawater, outperforming most previously reported g-C3N4-based photocatalysts. After 4-round cycling tests, there is no obvious decrease in H2 production activity and structural alternations, illustrating the high anti-corrosive capability of CDs/BCN-2 toward seawater. More importantly, large-scale synthesized CDs/BCN-20 photocatalyst also exhibits good photocatalytic performance, which further indicates the promising prospect of CDs/BCN applied in industrial catalysis.

CDs solution was fabricated through a typical hydrothermal method previously reported [29]. Briefly, 1.05 g of citric acid and 335 μL of ethylenediamine were add into 10 mL deionized water. Subsequently, the clarified solution was transferred into a 50 mL reactor and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the brown solution was filtered through a syringe filter (0.22 μm) and centrifuged for 30 min to remove impurities.

CDs/CN was synthesized via a traditional supramolecular self-assemble method on the basis of previous research [30]. In brief, 1.26 g of melamine, 1.29 g of cyanuric acid and 100 μL of fabricated CDs solution were dispersed in 50 mL deionized water and stirred overnight. Subsequently, the obtained suspension was centrifuged for 30 min and frozen-drying for 2 days. Finally, the prepared precursors were calcined at 550 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 5 °C min -1, named as CDs/CN. Pristine g-C3N4 (i.e. CN) was fabricated in the same way without adding CDs solution.

CDs/BCN was prepared by a thermal post-treatment of CDs/CN and the synthesis schematic was displayed in Fig. 1 . Firstly, 0.2 g of CDs/CN and a certain quantify of sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were completely ground and then calcined at 400 °C for 1 h with the ramping rate of 5 °C min -1 under an Ar atmosphere. The obtained samples were washed with deionized water and ethanol and subsequently dried overnight. As-prepared photocatalysts were denoted as CDs/BCN-x (x = 1, 2 and 3, representing 20%, 40% and 60% weight percentage of NaBH4 to CDs/CN, respectively).
Additional experimental details including materials, characterizations and the specific operation steps of photocatalytic hydrogen production tests could be found in the Supporting Information.

The schematic diagram of the preparation process and structure of CDs/BCN was exhibited in Fig. 1 (More details in experimental section), among which CDs/BCN was fabricated using a self-supramolecular reaction and NaBH4-assisted thermal reduction method. To put insight into the micromorphology of CN, CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-2, the scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) measurements were conducted. From Fig. S1, one can observe that both CN and CDs/CN possess large-sized flaky porous structure, indicating that loading CDs on the surface of CN has no impact on its morphology. On the Contrary, the introduction of B elements causes a dramatic change on the morphology of CDs/CN. As shown in Fig. 2 a and b, CDs/BCN-2 shows broken small-sized flaky porous structure and thus rougher and more wrinkled surface, mainly originating from the attack of the strong reductive H2 produced by NaBH4 [31,32]. To further confirm the successful anchoring of CDs, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was also carried out. As demonstrated in Fig. 2c, it can be clearly seen that CDs with the distribution between 1.3 and 3.4 nm are evenly distributed on the CN surface. Fig. 2d and e and Fig. S2 present a significant lattice spacing of 0.21 nm of CDs, which agrees well with the (001) crystal plane of graphite carbon [33]. In addition, the energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mappings provided by Fig. 2f–j verify the uniform distribution of C, N, O and B elements. All of the above characterization results confirm the successful introduction of CDs and B elements.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CN, CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-x were displayed in Fig. 3 a. All photocatalysts exhibit two well-defined characteristic peaks at 13.7° (100) and 27.3° (002), corresponding to the in-plane orderliness and the interlayer overlapping of tri-s-triazine unit, respectively [32,34,35]. Despite the crystal structures of CN, CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-x remain identical, the intensity of (100) and (002) diffraction peaks of CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-x become weaker compared to CN, suggesting the introduction of CDs and B elements disrupts the in-plane periodicity and framework orderliness of CN [17]. As exhibited in FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3b), all photocatalysts have almost identical feature peaks at 2900–3400, 1100–1750 and 814 cm-1, corresponding to the tensible vibrations of N–H, N–C heterocycles and heptazine rings, respectively [36,37]. Distinguished from CN and CDs/CN, CDs/BCN-x shows a new peak at around 2250 cm-1, which corresponds to the cyano group (–C≡N) stretching vibrations. The emergence of this new peak may be because NaBH4 as a strong reducing agent can generate active H2 during the thermal post-treatment process, which causes the decomposition of amino group (–NH2) and the breakage of C–NC bond, and finally yielding –C≡N [38]. Moreover, the N2 adsorption and desorption assays were carried out to obtain the BET specific surface areas and BJH pore-size distributions and the characterization results are shown in Fig. S3 and Table S1. All three samples have typical type Ⅳ isothermals with H3 hysteresis loop, indicating the generation of mesoporous structure [28,39]. As expected, the optimized photocatalysts i.e. CDs/BCN-2 have a large specific surface area of 82.80 m2/g with mesoporous structure, which can not only provide more photocatalytic reactive sites, but also offer faster mass transfer for HER, thus leading to higher photocatalytic performance in natural seawater.

Further, the chemical structures and electronic states of CN, CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-2 were obtained from X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). From Fig. S4 one can observe that all three samples contain three basic elements, i.e. C, N and O. It is noteworthy that a small amount of B elements appears in CDs/BCN-2 resulting from the B dopant introduced in the synthesis process. As shown in Fig. 4 a, two major peaks at 284.8 and 288.3 eV were observed in C 1s high-resolution spectra, representing C–C/CC and N–CN, respectively [40]. Note that CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-2 both contain a new peak of C–O bond at 286.6 eV compared to CN, which may be attributed to the abundant hydroxyl groups of CDs [2,17,36,41]. The N 1s high-resolution spectra in Fig. 4b display four peaks at 398.8, 400.1, 401.2 and 404.5 eV, corresponding to C–NC, N–C3, N–HX and π–excitations, respectively [42,43]. According to the fitting data shown in Table S2, the proportions of C–NC and N–HX in CDs/BCN-2 are lower than those of CDs/CN, consistent with the analysis results of FT-IR. This further proves the introduction of –C≡N as a combined result of the decomposition of –NH2 and the broken of C–NC. Interestingly, the O signals in CN (i.e. 532.2 and 533.5 eV) are detected from Fig. S5, probably caused by adsorbed molecules of oxygen-containing elements such as CO2, O2 and H2O. In comparison with CN, the peak intensity of CO of CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-2 slightly increases, possibly attributed to the role of oxygen-containing functional groups of CDs [34,44]. In addition, one can observe that the B 1s XPS spectra of CDs/BCN-2 appears a new peak at around 192.4 eV (Fig. 4c), belonging to the B–N bond [45–47]. To further determine the doping positions of B in CDs/BCN-2, we performed solid-state 11B NMR measurement. As exhibited in Fig. 4d, CDs/BCN-2 presents an obvious B signal at 13.34 ppm (the corner B, B1), and no characteristic signal at – 4.52 ppm (apex B, B2). The result indicates that B in CDs/BCN-2 may replace C of heptazine rings and accordingly occupy the B1 position [38]. The above data results and discussion confirm that B elements from NaBH4 have been successfully doped into the tri-s-triazine unit of CN.

The optical nature of CN, CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-2 were characterized by UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra (UV–vis DRS) (Fig. 5 a). Predictably, after loading CDs and doping B elements, CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-2 both exhibit stronger adsorption in the ultraviolet–visible range (220–800 nm). Moreover, compared with CN, the absorption spectra of CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-2 both have a significantly red shift and the absorption edge separately increases from 440 nm to 461 and 470 nm. Particularly, CDs/BCN-2 exhibits the strongest absorption ability in visible-light region originating from the unique upconverted photoluminescence property of CDs capable of capturing visible-light at different wavelengths as well as the optimized electronic structure of CDs/CN after doping B elements [45,48]. Subsequently, the band gap energy (Eg) of all samples was calculated by Kubelka-Munk method. As shown in Fig. 5b, one can observe that the Eg of CN is ∼2.63 eV, while that of CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-2 is narrowed to 2.58 and 2.51 eV, respectively. Obviously, the visible-light absorption capacity of CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-2 are further enhanced. In order to obtain the energy band structures of CN, CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-2, the valence band (VB) positions were obtained via high-resolution VB-XPS spectra, resulting in 1.90, 1.78 and 1.65 eV, respectively (Fig. 5c). Based on the above fitting results i.e. Eg and EVB values, the conduction band (CB) values of all three samples can be calculated to be −0.73, −0.80 and −0.86 eV, respectively (compared to normal hydrogen electrode (NHE)). Consequently, CDs/BCN-2 has the smallest Eg and the most negative CB position, leading to a wider spectral response and stronger thermodynamic driving force for the photocatalytic reduction of H+ to H2 [49]. Based on the above analysis, the energy band diagram of all three samples was shown in Fig. 5d.

In addition to the optical properties of photocatalysts, the transfer efficiency of photo-generated charge also plays an important role in the HER. Therefore, the photoelectrochemical properties of CN, CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-2 were investigated by the I-t curves as well as EIS plots. Compared with CN and CDs/CN, CDs/BCN-2 possesses the larger transient photocurrent response intensity, implying its effectively suppressed electron-hole pairs recombination (Fig. 6 a) [50,51]. EIS plots (Fig. 6b) shows that the impedance spectrum radius of CDs/BCN-2 is remarkably smaller than that of CN or CDs/CN, also proving that loading CDs and doping B elements can effectively reduce the transport resistance and improve the charge transfer efficiency [52,53]. Also, photoluminescence (PL) and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) were used to evaluate the separation efficiency and average lifetime of photo-induced carriers. As exhibited in Fig. 6c, the peak intensity of PL spectra of CN is the highest among all samples while that of CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-2 both decrease to some degree, indicating that the introduction of CDs and B elements is beneficial for the separation of electron-hole pairs [54]. The fitting results in Fig. 6d demonstrates that the average lifetime (τavg) of photo-generated carriers decreases from 4.88 ns (CN) to 3.32 ns (CDs/CN) to 2.53 ns (CDs/BCN-2), suggesting more efficient charge transfer occurring in CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-2 [55,56]. From all above experimental data and analysis results, it can be deduced that loading CDs and doping B elements into CN nanostructure can greatly improve its transfer and separation efficiency of photo-induced carriers beneficial to the enhancement of photocatalytic performance.

In order to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of CN, CDs/CN and CDs/BCN-x, the photocatalytic H2 production tests were carried out in natural seawater under visible-light activation (λ＞420 nm) using triethanolamine (TEOA) as a sacrificial agent and platinum (Pt) as a co-catalyst. For comparison, we initially synthesized GCN and PCN using dicyandiamide and melamine as precursors under the same calcination condition as CN (Fig. S6). It can be clearly seen that the H2 production performance of CN synthesized by supramolecular self-assembly method in natural seawater is dramatically improved to 1722 μmol g-1 h-1, which is attributed to the large specific surface area of 86.33 m2/g with flaky porous structure. Compared with the unmodified CN, the photocatalytic performance has been obviously improved due to the introduction of CDs and B elements, which effectively inhibit the recombination of photo-generated carriers and enhance the light capture capacity of CN in ultraviolet–visible region. As displayed in Fig. 7 a and b and Fig. S7, the optimal photocatalyst CDs/BCN-2 exhibits an outstanding H2 production rate of 10369 μmol g-1 h-1, which is 1.5, 2.6 and 6.0 folds better than that of CDs/CN (6743 μmol g-1 h-1), BCN (3978 μmol g-1 h-1) and CN (1722 μmol g-1 h-1). And the H2 production rate of the optimal CDs/BCN-2 in seawater still is competing in comparison with other previously reported g-C3N4-based photocatalysts (as depicted in Fig. 7c and Table S3). Note that the amount of CDs loaded on CN was experimentally optimized (Fig. S8) and the 3 wt% Pt loading amount was adopted considering the balance between cost and photocatalytic efficiency (Fig. S9).

Furthermore, the photocatalytic H2 production tests of CDs/BCN-2 under otherwise identical experimental conditions except for different salt solutions were also conducted to investigate the effects of various salt ions (Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl−, SO4 2−) presenting in natural seawater on photocatalytic performance. As shown in Fig. 7d, the H2 production rates of CDs/BCN-2 in NaCl, Na2SO4, CaCl2 and MgCl2 solutions reach to 10404, 9027, 8208 and 7573 μmol g-1 h-1, respectively. Such high-level H2 production rates of CDs/BCN-2 in various salt solutions can be reasonably explained based on the following several aspects: i) Na+ are readily adsorbed on g-C3N4 surface to create a new defect that allows the absorption of H+ to promote the photo-generated carriers transfer [15]; ii) Cl− may be oxidized by the photo-generated holes presenting in the VB of g-C3N4 to suppress the recombination of photo-induced carriers [57,58]; and iii) the inherent structure nature of synthesized CDs/BCN-2 can accelerate the diffusion of H2 and precipitates such as Mg(OH)2 and Ca(OH)2 formed during the HER to ensure the efficient photocatalytic performance [59,60]. Further, zeta potential measurements (Fig. S10) show that CDs/BCN-2 in seawater has the stronger adsorption capacity for H+ than it in deionized water. All of the above discussion indicates that CDs/BCN-2 with the highly-efficient H2 production performance without limitation of ions in natural seawater validates its universality for broad applications.

To characterize the properties of CDs/BCN-2 more clearly, we recorded the H2 production rates at different monochromic light wavelengths and calculated the corresponding apparent quantum yield (AQY) values. As displayed in Fig. 7e and Table S4, the H2 production rates and the values of AQY decrease gradually as the wavelength of monochromatic light increases and this tendency remains consistent with the absorption spectra of CDs/BCN-2, representing that the HER of CDs/BCN-2 is a light-driven reaction [61]. In practical applications, the stability also is a very important indicator to evaluate photocatalysts. As exhibited in Fig. 7f, 92% of original H2 production rate is remained even after 4 rounds of HER and no obvious alternations of CDs/BCN-2 in XRD patterns (Fig. 7g), FT-IR spectra (Fig. 7h) and SEM images (Fig. S11) before and after the cycling test appear, illustrating the superior stability and excellent corrosion resistance of CDs/BCN-2.

Since large-scale production of photocatalysts is essential for industrial catalysis, the photocatalytic activity of CDs/BCN-2 was further assessed by 20-fold amplification of precursor [62–64]. As exhibited in Fig. 8 a and b, the XRD patterns and FT-IR spectra among CN, CDs/BCN-2 and CDs/BCN-20 are essentially the same before and after amplification. More importantly, large-scale synthesized CDs/BCN-20 photocatalyst still maintains an excellent photocatalytic H2 production rate of 9022 μmol g-1 h-1 (Fig. 8c) and there is no significant decline of photocatalytic efficiency after 4 consecutive cycles (Fig. 8d). The yield of CDs/BCN-20 after 20-fold precursor amplification procedure still reaches 78.4%. All of the data results and discussion prove the possibility of the scalable synthesized CDs/BCN adapted to highly-efficient splitting seawater to produce H2.

According to the above results and discussion, a plausible mechanism for H2 production process in natural seawater occurring on CDs/BCN-2 was illustrated in Fig. 9 . After doping CDs and B elements into the nanostructure of CN, the Eg of CDs/BCN-2 is effectively narrowed (Fig. 5). Furthermore, CDs as electron traps may accelerate electron transfer of CDs/BCN-2 to the Pt sites (Fig. S12). Under the visible-light irradiation, more photo-induced carriers were generated in CDs/BCN-2 (Fig. 6). As the potential of the CB of CDs/BCN-2 is lower than that of H+/H2 (0 V vs. NHE), the photo-generated electrons can be trapped by H+ to form H2. At the same time, the photo-generated holes left on the VB are oxidized by TEOA to further inhibit the recombination of charge carriers. Eventually, all these factors work together on CDs/BCN-2 to achieve highly-efficient and stable H2 production performance in natural seawater.

In summary, the CDs/BCN photocatalyst with highly-efficient H2 production performance in natural seawater has been successfully fabricated via the NaBH4 thermal post-treatment of supramolecular self-assembled carbon nitride. The optimized CDs/BCN-2 exhibited flaky porous structure that allows providing more active sites and enabling faster mass transfer for HER. Meanwhile, doping CDs and B elements into CN effectively increased the utilization of visible-light and accelerated the transfer and separation of photo-generated carriers. Consequently, the optimal photocatalyst CDs/BCN-2 under visible-light irradiation exhibited outstanding H2 production activity of 10369 μmol g-1 h-1 in natural seawater, which is 6.0 and 1.5-time higher than that of CN (1722 μmol g-1 h-1) and CDs/CN (6743 μmol g-1 h-1). Furthermore, the satisfactory reusability and structural stability of CDs/BCN-2 in cycling tests demonstrated its excellent corrosion resistance in natural seawater. The high-level photocatalytic performance of CDs/BCN-2 in various salt solutions indicated its universality in practical applications. More importantly, CDs/BCN-20 photocatalyst synthesized via 20-fold precursor amplification also displayed satisfactory photocatalytic activity of 9022 μmol g-1 h-1 and excellent reusability. This work provides some insights for the scalable synthesis of photocatalysts with high performance and corrosion resistance in natural seawater.
Data will be made available on request.

Yaoze Gong: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Jun Huang: Investigation, Software. Zhi Zhang: Investigation. Zihu Kang: Resources. Xia Tao: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22176011 & 22378019) and the Opening Project of State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites (oic-202301008).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2024.05.017.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon defective g-C3N4 thin-wall tubes for drastic improvement of photocatalytic H2 production
Carbon
